retrieved_docs.title: ACE Inhibitors -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfonylureas: The combination of ACE inhibitors with sulfonylureas in diabetic patients may increase the risk of hypoglycemia. Regular monitoring of blood glucose is important when both are prescribed together.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluating glycemic control by checking HbA1c levels is recommended every 3 months during each follow-up visit. Other laboratory tests that should be conducted, if not performed within the past year, include a lipid profile, serum creatinine, spot urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio, liver function tests, thyroid-stimulating hormone, complete blood count with platelets, and serum potassium—especially if the patient is also taking an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor, angiotensin receptor blocker, or diuretic. Since people with T1D are at an increased risk of developing other autoimmune diseases, such as autoimmune thyroid conditions, celiac disease, primary adrenal insufficiency, and rheumatoid arthritis, screening for autoimmune disorders should be considered when clinically appropriate. [18]

retrieved_docs.content: Fungal infection Delirium Acidosis Hyperglycemia Peripheral ischemia

retrieved_docs.title: ACE Inhibitors -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: ACE inhibitors may delay the progression of nephropathy and reduce the risks of cardiovascular events in hypertensive patients with diabetes mellitus type I and type II. The addition of an ACE inhibitor has shown a significant reduction in serum creatinine in hypertensive and normotensive patients with albuminuria. ACE inhibitor therapy has shown a significant decrease in the progression of diabetic nephropathy. [10] [11] Krecak I. et al. found evidence that ACE inhibitors might have renoprotective properties in patients with polycythemia vera. [12] Yao J.et al. discovered that ACE inhibitors improve insulin sensitivity in hypertensive patients with diabetes mellitus compared to angiotensin II receptor blockers. [13] Salmenkari H. et al. found evidence that the use of ACE inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor blockers in patients with inflammatory bowel disease results in fewer hospitalizations, glucocorticoid use, and a milder disease course. [14] The treatment with ACE inhibitors in adult patients with the diagnosis of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), now also called metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), especially those with associated chronic kidney disease, is associated with decreased risk of liver-related events (LRE), defined as the risk of cirrhosis complications and liver cancer. [15] [16] Hypertensive patients with Alzheimer's disease (AD) treated with ACE inhibitors demonstrated intact physical capacity, improved hand grip strength, and prevention of neuromuscular junction degradation, as indicated by the maintenance of plasma C-terminal agrin fragment-22 (CAF22) levels. [17] Similar findings of better hand grip strength and gait speed, but low CAF22 levels, were also noted in CHF patients treated with ACE inhibitors. [18] ACE inhibitors are also used in scleroderma renal crisis. [19] [20] A recent study found that renin-angiotensin system (RAS) blockers, including ACE inhibitors, provided similar mortality benefits in both Black and non-Black patients with heart failure and reduced ejection fraction. Although the relative reduction in heart failure hospitalizations was smaller in Black patients, the absolute benefit was comparable due to their higher baseline risk. [21] The meta-analysis suggests that renin-angiotensin system inhibitors, including ACE inhibitors, are associated with a reduced mortality rate in patients with atrial fibrillation; however, further large-scale trials are needed to confirm these findings. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Somogyi Phenomenon -- Clinical Significance -- Hypoglycemia vs. Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperglycemic episodes are also to be avoided. Symptoms to look out for include polyuria, polydipsia, weakness, tachycardia, and hypotension. [11] The hyperglycemia will worsen the cytokine and inflammatory response, causing even more hyperglycemia. [11] Consequences of hyperglycemia include renal failure, polyneuropathy, retinopathy, and arrhythmias. [11] Hyperglycemia produces an increase in advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which deposit in the body, causing connective tissue crosslinking, fibrosis, and impaired relaxation of the heart. [12] People with diabetes become insulin-resistant, which keeps blood glucose levels elevated, causing an even more increase in AGEs. [12] With the failure to keep blood glucose levels at a normal range, the hyperglycemic episodes also induce the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), increasing vascular resistance and arterial pressure in the body. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Treatment / Management -- Hypoglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia is the most frequent adverse effect of insulin therapy. Educating people living with T1D and their partners about the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia is crucial. Symptoms include sweating, rapid heartbeat, lightheadedness, confusion, hunger, visual changes, and tremors. Symptoms of hypoglycemia typically occur when blood glucose levels drop below 70 mg/dL, known as level 1 hypoglycemia. Level 2 hypoglycemia occurs when blood glucose levels fall below 54 mg/dL and is usually associated with cognitive impairment and altered consciousness. Level 3 hypoglycemia involves a hypoglycemic episode that requires assistance from another person for resuscitation.

retrieved_docs.title: Aspart Insulin -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Antidiabetic agents, ACE inhibitors, fluoxetine, fibrates, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, salicylates, and octreotide can increase the risk of hypoglycemia. When using insulin aspart in conjunction with these medications, increasing the frequency of glucose monitoring is advised. Drugs that may reduce the blood glucose-lowering effect of insulin aspart include corticosteroids, diuretics, estrogens, niacin, oral contraceptives, protease inhibitors, thyroid hormone, albuterol, epinephrine, and atypical antipsychotics (eg, olanzapine, clozapine). [20] Dose adjustment of insulin and more frequent glucose monitoring may be required. Pentamidine may increase or decrease the blood glucose-reducing effect of insulin aspart. When insulin aspart is concomitantly administered with pentamidine, dose adjustments may be necessary. Beta-blockers and clonidine may mask the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia and should be used with caution. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.content: Remain compliant with medication; insulin omission is a leading cause of DKA

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes in Children -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Carry sugar snacks in case of hypoglycemia

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin/insulin receptor antibodies: Hypoglycaemia occurs with elevated insulin levels and incompletely suppressed C-peptide levels. Insulin levels are more markedly elevated in those with insulin antibodies, whilst they are mildly elevated in those with insulin receptor antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Independent of insulin: Insulin independent hypoglycemia includes alcohol, visceral failure (liver/renal failure), critical illness, primary adrenal failure, anterior pituitary failure, severe sepsis, cerebral malaria, anorexia nervosa, glycogen storage disease, post-bariatric surgery, mesenchymal tumors with elevated IGF-2 levels, autoimmune hypoglycemia secondary to anti-insulin or anti-insulin receptor antibodies and drugs. Hypoglycemia caused by drugs is very common . Many drugs other than anti-hyperglycemic medications and alcohol have been reported. Common medications include quinolones, anti-malarial, glucagon, lithium, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor antagonists, non-selective B-blockers, etc.). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Aspart Insulin -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Critically ill patients receiving insulin aspart should have their blood glucose level tested every 1 to 2 hours. Non-critically ill patients on insulin aspart should routinely monitor their blood glucose concentration at home or in the hospital to assess the efficacy of the insulin dose. This testing ideally occurs before or 2 hours after a meal. Insulin dose adjustments should be based on testing results, typically 10% to 20% in either direction. All patients on insulin therapy should have their HgbA1c tested every 6 months and electrolyte levels tested annually. Hemoglobin A1c should be monitored quarterly in patients who do not meet treatment goals or after changes in therapy. According to the American Diabetes Association, monitoring goals include a fasting blood glucose level of 80 to 130 mg/dL, a peak postprandial (1 to 2 hours post-meal) blood glucose level of less than 180 mg/dL, and a HgbA1c level less than 7.0% for non-pregnant adult patients. These goals may change for individual patients based on age, duration of diabetes, comorbid conditions, hypoglycemia unawareness, risk of a hypoglycemic event, and other considerations. Patients with more comorbid conditions or a higher risk of harm during a hypoglycemic event have less stringent goals. According to the Endocrine Society guidelines, patients with type 1 diabetes who receive multiple daily injections should receive continuous glucose monitoring instead of self-monitoring via fingerstick. Continuous glucose monitoring should be initiated in the inpatient setting for patients who are at high risk for hypoglycemia. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: Episodes of hyperglycemia over an extended period may lead to either diabetic ketoacidosis or a hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state. Untreated hyperglycemia may cause the client to enter ketoacidosis, using fatty acids for gluconeogenesis to produce the energy required for cellular function. Ketoacidosis may be due to decreased insulin production, as seen in type 1 diabetes mellitus; this clinical condition is termed diabetic ketoacidosis and is a life-threatening scenario. The lack of insulin production may result in high serum levels of ketones and ketoacids. Symptoms of diabetic ketoacidosis may include fruity breath, ketonuria, tachypnea, tachycardia, dyspnea, nausea, vomiting, altered mentation, and coma.

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: The etiologies of hyperglycemia include but are not limited to: Inadequate insulin administration in clients with type 1 diabetes mellitus Insulin resistance with or without frank type 2 diabetes mellitus [25] Stress-related experiences such as critical illness [26] The dawn phenomena - a hormone surge between 4 am and 5 am that causes a spike in blood glucose levels [27]

Insulin aspart (human) binds the INSR protein and is used therapeutically for type 1 and type 2 diabetes, but is contraindicated in hypoglycemia to avoid further glucose lowering. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia and hyperosmolality are classified within fluid, electrolyte and acid‑base disorders, a subset of metabolic disorders that intersect with other endocrine/metabolic/nutritional diseases and carbohydrate metabolism disorder. These conditions are linked to plasma hyperosmolality and severe hyperosmolar states such as diabetic hyperosmolar coma, and they commonly coexist with electrolyte abnormalities including hyperkalemia, increased anion gap, hyperuricemia, hypochloremia, and with acid‑base disturbances such as acidosis and respiratory alkalosis. High plasma osmolality is closely associated with hyperglycemia, hyperketonemia, and both type 1 diabetes and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, which are treated by targeting the INSR protein with rapid‑acting insulin analogues such as insulin aspart; however, insulin aspart is contraindicated in hypoglycemia. Endocrine pancreatic disease manifestations include hyperglycemia, elevated blood ketones, hyperinsulinemia,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), and abnormal psychological states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). This interrelationship indicates that individuals with type 1 diabetes are at increased risk of developing acute kidney failure, potentially mediated by hypovolemia, and that the onset of acute kidney failure may be accompanied by psychological disturbances. Recognizing and addressing both volume status and mental health in patients with type 1 diabetes can help prevent or mitigate acute kidney failure and improve overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 197
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart (human) exerts a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis and acts on the INSR protein (human) as a molecular target. This rapid‑acting insulin analogue is employed in the treatment of both مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes) and مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes). Clinical observations link both نوع 1 and نوع 2 diabetes to an increased risk of فشل كلوي حاد (acute kidney failure) and to ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (elevated plasma osmolality). Acute kidney failure frequently co‑occurs with dehydration (التجفاف), abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية), low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), and is especially associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين). Elevated plasma osmolality is also clinically associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes and abnormal mental states, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume. Dehydration is a physiological precursor that maps to elevated plasma osmolality, reflecting the close relationship between fluid loss and osmotic imbalance. Both فشل كلوي حاد and ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما are categorized under اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder), indicating that they are specific manifestations within the broader spectrum of urinary tract pathology. Together, these interconnections highlight how insulin therapy, diabetes, renal dysfunction, fluid‑electrolyte disturbances, and neuro‑psychiatric manifestations are intertwined, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management in patients with diabetes‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with these forms of diabetes are at increased risk for developing sudden renal impairment. Dehydration (التجفاف) is also clinically linked to acute kidney failure, serving as a common precipitating factor that can exacerbate renal dysfunction in diabetic individuals. Recognizing the interplay among acute kidney failure, diabetes (both type 1 and insulin‑dependent type 2), and dehydration is essential for early identification, prevention, and management of renal complications in these patient populations.

The urinary tract disorder may raise plasma osmolality, which in turn is associated with dehydration, low blood volume, insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, and altered mental state. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1570
retrieved_docs.content: Akarbóza is classified as a hypoglykemika and is used for lowering elevated blood glucose levels. Insulin Lispro, in turn, is a specific type of hypoglykemika, belonging to the insulin class of medications that act to reduce blood sugar. Both akarbóza and insulin Lispro are therapeutic agents employed in the management of hyperglycemia, particularly in patients with diabetes, and they share the common function of decreasing circulating glucose concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
retrieved_docs.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) comprises hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), an increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية), hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), hyponatremia syndrome (متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم), and hypochloremia (نقص كلوريد الدم). Insulin aspart, human (insulin aspart, human) decreases lipolysis, binds the insulin receptor protein (INSR protein, human), and is indicated for type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2) but is contraindicated when blood glucose is low (نقص تركيز السكر). Diseases of the endocrine pancreas, classified under other endocrine disorders, include diabetes without complications (مرض السكري دون مضاعفات), type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر), elevated blood ketones in diabetic patients (ارتفاع الكيتونات في الدم عند مرضى السكري), hyperinsulinemia (فرط أنسولين الدم), glucagonoma (ورم غلوكاغوني), and disorders of gastroincretin secretion (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين). Elevated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 117
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified as a child of fluid‑and‑electrolyte disorders (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات), a subgroup of metabolic disorders that also includes electrolyte imbalance, carbohydrate metabolism disorder, hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم), hypokalemia syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), increased anion gap, hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), hyponatremia syndrome (متلازمة نقص صوديوم الدم), and hypochloremia, all of which are characterized by increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما). Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with hyperglycemia, type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2), and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, while hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is further linked to hyperkalemia. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1059
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, human is employed as a therapeutic agent for both مرض السكري نوع 1 and مرض السكري نوع 2, reflecting its role in managing the two major forms of diabetes that belong to the broader category DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES, but it is contraindicated in the presence of نقص تركيز السكر. DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES, classified under other endocrine disorders, encompasses child conditions such as ارتفاع السكر في الدم, ارتفاع الكيتونات في الدم عند مرضى السكري, نقص تركيز السكر, مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, اضطراب إفراز الجسترين, اعتلال عصبي سكري, ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. Clinically, ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما is closely associated with ارتفاع السكر في الدم, ارتفاع الكيتونات في الدم عند مرضى السكري, and مرض السكري نوع 1, underscoring osmotic disturbances that accompany uncontrolled hyperglycemia and ketonemia, particularly in type 1 diabetes. ارتفاع الكيتونات في الدم عند مرضى السكري is linked to

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1302
retrieved_docs.content: Acute hypernatremia is classified as a fluid and electrolyte disorder, a subset of electrolyte imbalance that raises plasma osmolality and can precipitate diabetic hyperosmolar coma. Electrolyte imbalance also includes increased anion gap, hyperuricemia, hyponatremia syndrome, hypochloremia, and hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) within the broader category of other endocrine disorders. Diseases of the endocrine pancreas encompass diabetes mellitus and related conditions such as hyperglycemia, elevated blood ketones in diabetic patients, type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes (including insulin‑requiring type 2), diabetic neuropathy, hyperinsulinemia, progressive hemifacial atrophy, disorders of gastrin secretion, and the rare glucagon‑producing tumor (ورم غلوكاغوني).
